summary: Gruber offers a thorough defense of Apple's Smart Battery Case, arguing that its unusual hump design reflects deliberate engineering trade-offs rather than poor design. He outlines Apple's likely priority list — battery life, hand feel, ease of use, protection, antenna performance, and looks (last) — and argues the case feels better in hand than any competitor despite looking odd. He dismisses patent-avoidance theories as nonsense, citing a source. He critiques reviewers who compare raw mAh capacity without understanding the intended use case: getting power users through a single heavy day, not multi-day off-grid trips. Gruber concludes that if you need extra battery every day, get an iPhone 6S Plus, but for occasional heavy-use days, Apple's case is the clear best battery case available.
key_points: Apple deliberately prioritized feel, usability, and antenna performance over appearance — an unusual ordering for Apple, but rational given that no battery case has ever been elegant
The hump design may partly serve vanity for the iPhone itself, making it obvious the phone inside is thin rather than hiding it in uniform bulk
Comparing raw mAh capacity between cases misses the point: the Smart Battery Case is designed to double a single day's use, not replace wall power for days
Tim Cook's overnight hiking justification was off-message — the real use case is power users who drain their phones during heavy daily use
Gruber's core wish is that Apple had made the iPhone 6/6S slightly thicker with a bigger internal battery, eliminating most users' need for any external battery
money_quotes: That 'looks good' is last on the list is unusual for an Apple product, to say the least.
Whether you like it, hate it, or are ambivalent about it, this is the battery case Apple wanted to make.
My take is that the Smart Battery Case is an inelegant design, but it is solving a problem for which, to date, no one has created an elegant solution.
Nothing could better encapsulate the wrong way of looking at the Smart Battery Case than this tweet.

Complaining about the lack of an on/off toggle or external charge capacity indicator lights on the Smart Battery Case reminds me of the complaints about the original iPhone omitting the then-ubiquitous green/red hardware buttons for starting and ending phone calls.
If Apple doesn't print anything on the front face of the iPhone, why in the world would a case maker?

The clear lesson is that it's far better to give a phone more battery life by making the phone itself thicker and including a correspondingly thicker internal battery than by using any sort of external battery.
However bulky the Plus feels in your pocket and hands, it feels less bulky to me than the iPhone 6S with any battery pack.
key_insight: Apple's Smart Battery Case makes different trade-offs than competitors — prioritizing feel and simplicity over appearance and raw capacity — and those trade-offs are defensible even if the result looks odd.
